result:
I keep coming back to the idea that attention is the only non-renewable currency. Not time—time is just the ledger on which attention is spent. You can “save” an hour, but if your mind was elsewhere, the hour vanishes anyway, like a theater ticket left in a pocket and washed. What you actually possessed was the beam of awareness you chose to shine on a face, a sentence, a blade of grass. Once redirected, that particular beam can never be recovered; the photons are gone, the moment’s retina overwritten.

This is why I mistrust efficiency apps that promise to “give” me time. They give me empty squares on a calendar, but they cannot give me the soft focus I once had at dusk when I watched swallows stitch the sky over the barn and felt, without words, that the world was a net holding me. The calendar square can be filled with another meeting, another scroll, another podcast, but it cannot be filled with that swallow-barn dusk unless I am willing to pay the real price: ignoring everything else.

I’ve started cheating on the exchange rate. I leave my phone at home and walk the long way to the post office, letting the dog choose turns. He is a connoisseur of smells; every fire hydrant is a newspaper older than print. I try to read over his shoulder, translating odor into story: the ghost of a female beagle in heat, the fryer oil from the Korean taco truck, the electric tang of a squirrel’s terror. The sniff is the dog’s beam of attention, and for a few blocks I borrow it, trading vision for olfaction, like swapping lenses on a camera. The world goes synesthetic—curbs taste of last night’s rain, the birch tree smells silver. I arrive at the post office with no memory of the route, pockets empty of thoughts, the way a sleeper wakes with no memory of the pillow.

There is a secret society of people who live this theft daily. The bartender who spends an extra thirty seconds polishing the same spot on the glass while a queue forms, just to feel the sun angle through the window and warm her wrist. The grandmother who pretends to look for her reading glasses while she studies the reflection of clouds in the blank TV screen. Children before we colonize them with clocks. We recognize one another by the hesitation between tasks, the pause that costs us nothing on paper but everything in the ledger of elsewhere.

I want to speak them a password, but the only phrase that feels honest is “I was here.” Not “I am here”—that’s declarative, instagrammable. “I was here” is past tense, elegiac, the admission that the moment is already gone and I paid full price for it. I whisper it to the dog when he finally lifts his nose from the grass. His tail wags once, agreement or consolation, and we step back into the traffic of beams.
